skill.name: mcp-builder
skill.description: Guides developers through planning, implementing, testing, and evaluating Model Context Protocol servers for Python FastMCP and Node or TypeScript SDK projects.
skill.summary: Build and evaluate Python or TypeScript MCP servers with workflow-focused design guidance.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical patterns, but review found most are markdown examples, documentation URLs, or prose false positives. The meaningful risk is that bundled evaluation scripts can launch user-supplied local MCP server commands and connect to user-supplied remote MCP endpoints with headers or environment values. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Evaluation harness can launch user-specified MCP server commands
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for elevated local execution risk. The evaluation CLI accepts a command, arguments, and environment variables, then passes them to the MCP stdio connection. This is expected for testing local MCP servers, but users should only run trusted commands and evaluation files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command and argument flow is explicit in the CLI and connection wrapper. It is legitimate evaluation functionality, so the concern is misuse rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Remote MCP evaluation accepts arbitrary URLs and custom headers
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for elevated network and credential-handling risk. The evaluation script accepts SSE or HTTP URLs and user-provided headers, including authorization examples. This is normal for remote MCP evaluation, but it can expose credentials if pointed at an untrusted endpoint.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL and header handling is directly visible. The behavior supports the stated tool purpose, so the risk depends on operator trust and endpoint choice.

low_findings.title: Markdown code fences were misclassified as shell execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for most static backtick findings. The repeated hits occur in documentation code fences, inline command examples, and XML examples rather than executable Ruby or shell backtick calls in runtime code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown examples and documentation references. They are not parsed as runtime code by the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detections appear to be prose false positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Reviewed examples flagged as weak cryptographic algorithms are documentation headings, naming examples, or MCP prose, with no cryptographic API usage or hashing implementation found in those contexts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled static locations are instructional text, not crypto code. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar weak algorithms being used by runtime scripts.

low_findings.title: Documentation instructs web fetching of public MCP resources
low_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for network dependency, not malicious exfiltration. The skill asks agents to fetch public MCP and SDK documentation during research. This is aligned with the skill purpose, but users should expect external network access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network access instructions are explicit and point to public documentation. No evidence found that these instructions send local secrets or private data.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-controlled command is passed to MCP stdio transport
dangerous_patterns.description: The evaluation helper forwards CLI command, args, and env values into the stdio transport. This pattern can execute local programs and should be limited to trusted MCP servers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The data path from CLI parameters to stdio server parameters is direct. The pattern is intentional testing infrastructure rather than covert execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Custom headers can be sent to user-selected remote MCP endpoints
dangerous_patterns.description: The remote evaluation path accepts a URL and headers, then passes them to SSE or streamable HTTP clients. This can transmit bearer tokens or other secrets to the selected endpoint.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL and header pass-through is clear. The behavior is useful for evaluation, but unsafe endpoint selection would expose credentials.

content.value_statement: MCP servers are hard to design when tools mirror raw APIs instead of real workflows. This skill guides planning, implementation, testing, and evaluation for Python and TypeScript MCP servers.
actual_capabilities: Guides workflow-focused MCP tool design instead of simple endpoint wrapping.
Provides Python FastMCP implementation patterns with Pydantic validation guidance.
Provides Node and TypeScript MCP SDK patterns with Zod schema guidance.
Defines testing steps for syntax checks, builds, and safe server execution.
Includes an evaluation workflow for XML question sets and Claude-based scoring.
Covers pagination, response formats, annotations, and actionable error messages.
limitations: Does not generate a complete MCP server without service-specific API documentation.
Requires network access when fetching current MCP or SDK documentation.
Evaluation scripts can run local commands, so users must supply trusted command values.
Examples are templates and must be adapted to each service authorization model.

use_cases.title: Plan a Service Integration
use_cases.description: Map a service API into agent-friendly MCP workflows before writing implementation code.

use_cases.title: Implement a Typed MCP Server
use_cases.description: Build Python or TypeScript MCP tools with validation, clear descriptions, and consistent response formats.

use_cases.title: Evaluate Tool Quality
use_cases.description: Create stable read-only evaluation questions and run them against an MCP server.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Tools
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new MCP integration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to plan an MCP server for [service]. Identify the top workflows, proposed tool names, inputs, outputs, and safety annotations.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Python Server
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a Python FastMCP implementation outline.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder Python guidance to design a FastMCP server for [service]. Include Pydantic input models, shared API helpers, error handling, and response formats.

prompt_templates.title: Draft TypeScript Server
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a Node or TypeScript MCP implementation outline.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder TypeScript guidance to design a server for [service]. Include project structure, Zod schemas, tool registration, build steps, and annotations.

prompt_templates.title: Build Evaluations
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced quality testing for an implemented MCP server.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder evaluation guidance to create ten stable read-only questions for this MCP server. Include verification notes and expected answers.

output_examples.input: Plan an MCP server for a project management API.
output: A prioritized workflow list for projects, tasks, comments, and users.
Tool names with input fields, validation rules, and response format choices.
A testing checklist for build, syntax, and evaluation coverage.

output_examples.input: Review my MCP tool descriptions for agent usability.
output: Findings on unclear names, missing parameter guidance, and overly broad tools.
Suggested wording for descriptions and error messages.
Recommendations for pagination, truncation, and annotations.

output_examples.input: Create evaluation questions for a read-only analytics MCP server.
output: Ten realistic questions with stable expected answers.
Notes on which tools each question should exercise.
Warnings for any question that depends on changing data.

best_practices: Start by designing complete user workflows, then map them to MCP tools.
Use strict input validation and concise outputs to protect agent context budget.
Run evaluation tasks with trusted servers, trusted endpoints, and limited credentials.
anti_patterns: Do not expose every API endpoint as a separate MCP tool without workflow design.
Do not return large raw payloads when a summarized or paginated response is enough.
Do not run the evaluation harness with untrusted commands, URLs, headers, or XML files.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you build MCP servers that expose useful tools for agents and external services.

faq.question: Does it support Python and TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for Python FastMCP and the Node or TypeScript MCP SDK.

faq.question: Does it create production credentials or hosting?
faq.answer: No. You must provide service credentials, deployment choices, and environment-specific configuration.

faq.question: Why does the skill mention web fetching?
faq.answer: It asks agents to fetch current MCP and SDK documentation during research.

faq.question: Is the included evaluation harness safe?
faq.answer: It is useful for trusted tests, but it can run supplied commands and send supplied headers to remote URLs.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
